Meaning: small design or information changes that guide tourists towards lower-impact choices

151. __________ differs by neighbourhood.

Meaning: resident tolerance for visitor pressure

152. Tourism’s __________ includes transport.

Meaning: environmental impact of activity

153. __________ can simplify traditions.

Meaning: turning culture into a commodity

154. Licensing data are essential for credible __________.

Meaning: the practical execution and enforcement of destination policy

155. Long-term competitiveness depends on place quality.

Meaning: ability to attract and satisfy visitors

156. Timed tickets can space arrivals out across the day.

Meaning: arrange visits at intervals or across places to reduce concentration

157. Cities can __________ cruise calls during the busiest months.

Meaning: reduce an activity carefully rather than stop it suddenly

158. Authorities can roll a visitor levy in gradually.

Meaning: introduce a rule or system in planned stages

159. A city can __________ licences in streets under severe pressure.

Meaning: reduce or remove a policy, permit or activity over time

160. Managers can cap visits at a safe level.

Meaning: set a maximum number

161. Visitors may need to __________.

Meaning: reserve before arrival

162. Policies may encourage visitors to __________.

Meaning: spend the night

163. Local firms can __________ off-season cultural events.

Meaning: expand into a different activity, season or visitor market

164. Tourism policy should __________ from volume targets.

Meaning: shift from an approach

165. Maintenance costs __________ when visitor numbers exceed site capacity.

Meaning: increase gradually until the total becomes significant

166. Tourist uses may __________ local services.

Meaning: displace residents or activities

167. Heavy footfall can __________ historic surfaces.

Meaning: damage through repeated use

168. A new booking system can __________ calmer visitor flows.

Meaning: introduce a new policy or period of change

169. Regenerative tourism should __________ to communities.

Meaning: contribute to a place

170. Cities may __________ on illegal rentals.

Meaning: enforce rules strongly

Integrated original synthesis

4. Original reading: A destination is a place before it is a product

Read for connections: crowd concentration, resident consent, local value, heritage protection, visitor dispersal, seasonal balance and destination governance.

1 · A destination is a home before it is a product

Tourism is both an economic activity and a use of place. Visitors buy accommodation, transport, food and culture, while residents continue to work, study, shop and maintain relationships in the same streets. Conflict arises when policy measures success mainly through tourist arrivals and ignores whether the destination remains liveable. Overtourism is therefore not simply “too many tourists.” It is a condition in which visitor activity exceeds the physical, ecological or social capacity of a place.

National statistics can conceal this problem. A country may absorb millions of visitors comfortably while one historic centre, beach or monument experiences extreme tourism intensity. Pressure also varies by hour and season. Cruise passengers may arrive together, social-media trends may direct everyone towards one viewpoint, and peak-season demand may overwhelm transport and waste systems that remain underused during winter. Effective policy needs data at neighbourhood and site level.

The concept of carrying capacity is useful but easily oversimplified. Physical capacity concerns how many people fit safely. Ecological capacity concerns erosion, wildlife disturbance, water and waste. Social carrying-capacity concerns the point at which residents or visitors experience unacceptable loss of quality. These limits are not fixed numbers. They change with infrastructure, behaviour, climate and management.

Visitor management includes timed entry, routes, information, pricing and reservation. A fragile monument may require visitors to book ahead, while paths and barriers protect the heritage fabric. A natural area may introduce visitor caps or seasonal closure. Such measures work best when the reason is clear and when alternatives exist for people without smartphones, advance knowledge or flexible travel dates.

Averages conceal local pressure. Tourism intensity and visitor pressure should be measured street by street and month by month, then compared with practical carrying capacity. That evidence allows visitor management and destination management to respond before daily life or heritage fabric deteriorates.

2 · Pressure depends on concentration, timing and behaviour

Crowding is also an urban land-use issue. Tourism accommodation may generate higher returns than long-term renting, encouraging landlords to convert homes into holiday rentals or hotels. Large concentrations of short-term rentals can weaken housing affordability, remove stable residents and alter building management. Souvenir shops and luggage services may crowd out groceries, schools and ordinary services.

The response should distinguish occasional home sharing from professional accommodation portfolios. Registration, safety rules, tax reporting and platform data allow cities to identify commercial activity. Authorities may crack down on illegal rentals or wind back new tourism-accommodation permits in saturated districts. Without enforcement, limits exist mainly as announcements, a policy category in which human institutions have achieved remarkable expertise.

Tourism still creates substantial value. The visitor economy supports hospitality, transport, museums, food production and retail. Visitor spending can sustain businesses that local demand alone would not support. Tourism receipts provide tax revenue and foreign exchange. The central question is whether destinations retain enough value to compensate for public and social costs.

Tourism leakage occurs when income leaves through foreign-owned hotels, cruise companies, booking platforms or imported supplies. Stronger local value-capture comes from local ownership, fair wages, procurement and longer stays. Visitors who stay over usually buy more services than day visitors, while independent travellers may distribute spending differently from packaged groups.

Economic success also needs a local test. A large visitor economy may still create tourism dependence, while imported ownership and purchasing increase tourism leakage. Policies that strengthen local value-capture and improve seasonal employment make visitor spending more useful to host communities.

3 · Residents must share both decisions and value

Employment quality matters. Tourism creates entry-level and flexible work, but seasonal employment can mean unstable hours, low wages and housing difficulty. Extending the season may improve continuity, although communities and ecosystems also need recovery periods. A policy that merely expands insecure work from four months to ten has improved business utilisation more clearly than worker welfare.

Cruise tourism demonstrates concentration. One vessel can deliver thousands of passengers within hours. This may usher in a new revenue stream, yet streets, ports and heritage sites experience sudden pressure. Much spending remains on board, and cruise emissions affect coastal air and climate. Cities can coordinate schedules, raise port fees, provide shore power or ease back on arrivals when capacity is exceeded.

Fees are increasingly used at iconic places. Visitor fees and tourist taxes may finance maintenance, toilets, transport or conservation funding. They can also influence demand if prices vary by season or booking time. However, a fee does not automatically solve crowding. It may simply convert an overcrowded public place into an overcrowded paid place.

Fairness requires exemptions, resident access and transparency. Cultural and natural heritage often carries public meaning beyond the tourism market. Equitable access may involve free days, school programmes or lower charges for local residents. Fee revenue should be audited and linked to visible outcomes, otherwise the policy loses public confidence.

Demand can be reshaped rather than merely reduced. Managing peak-season demand through seasonal dispersal, spatial dispersal and wider visitor dispersal can protect crowded centres. However, destination marketing must not transfer pressure to places without transport, waste systems or resident consent.

4 · Heritage needs management, not empty preservation

Tourism and heritage have a complicated relationship. Cultural tourism can finance restoration, interpretation and traditional skills. Visitors create attention that strengthens political support for protection. At the same time, excessive footfall can wear down surfaces, while commercial performance may simplify rituals and crafts into products shaped by outsider expectations.

Cultural authenticity should not mean freezing communities in an imagined past. Cultures change, and residents may choose to adapt traditions for new audiences. The problem is power. Community consent matters when businesses use cultural knowledge, sacred places or performances. Local participants should control what is shared and receive fair payment.

UNESCO’s sustainable-tourism approach places heritage values at the centre of planning. Heritage conservation should determine the acceptable tourism model, not become a repair service for damage created by unlimited promotion. Site conservation, visitor interpretation and emergency planning require skilled staff and stable budgets.

Climate change adds further pressure. Heat, wildfire, floods, erosion and sea-level rise threaten both cultural and natural attractions. Heritage resilience requires visitor-pressure risk appraisal, drainage, fire planning and documentation. Tourism businesses also depend on these assets, so climate adaptation should be funded across the destination rather than left entirely to conservation agencies.

Social legitimacy is measurable. Falling resident sentiment, weak community consent and visible local displacement signal that growth has crossed a political limit. Protecting cultural authenticity and funding heritage conservation keep a destination distinctive because residents still live and create there.

5 · Better tourism changes the pattern of demand

Wildlife-based tourism faces similar contradictions. Visitors can finance protected areas, yet roads, beach grooming, noise and water demand damage habitats. The destination’s environmental footprint includes transport to the site, not only hotel recycling. A beach cannot be called sustainable merely because guests reuse towels while nesting areas are mechanically cleared for photographs.

Dispersal is a common response to overtourism. Spatial dispersal promotes secondary districts or nearby towns, while seasonal dispersal encourages off-season travel. Authorities can space out flows and help regions diversify into new tourism products. The approach may improve visitor experience and support economic diversification.

Yet dispersal can export the problem. Smaller destinations may lack waste collection, transport, housing or political capacity. Promotion creates demand faster than services can be built. Destination marketing should therefore follow local preparation and community consent. A place should be able to decline promotion or pause growth.

Technology can support this work. Smart tickets, crowd sensors and mobile information help people avoid congestion. Booking systems allocate access and provide advance data. Digital tools also create privacy and accessibility concerns, and platforms may control valuable information. Public data governance is necessary so destinations do not become dependent on private companies for knowledge about their own visitors.

The wider shift is from marketing to destination stewardship. Traditional tourism agencies focused on attracting more people. Stewardship considers resident wellbeing, environmental limits, cultural continuity and visitor quality. Resident sentiment should be measured alongside occupancy and spending.

A stronger tourism strategy uses several indicators: pressure by place and time, local ownership, employment quality, housing impact, emissions and heritage condition. It seeks regenerative tourism where visitors and businesses give back through restoration, learning or community finance. Regeneration should not become another decorative label; the claimed benefit must be measurable.

Tourism will remain valuable and desirable. Travel supports learning, exchange and livelihoods. The policy challenge is to preserve these benefits without allowing visitor demand to reorganise every home, street and tradition around temporary consumption. A successful destination is not one that receives the maximum possible number of tourists. It is a place that remains worth living in and, because of that, worth visiting.

Tools should match the problem. Visitor fees can fund maintenance; timed entry and visitor caps can protect fragile sites; and tourism zoning can defend residential streets. The wider objective is regenerative tourism that improves the place rather than simply reducing obvious damage.

Continue to model essays

Idea-building model

5. Advanced C2 essay

Question: Should residents have the right to limit tourism even when visitor growth would increase local income?
Extended model · 1670 words · designed to build arguments, not imitate exam length

Tourism can create employment, tax revenue and international visibility. For places with few alternative industries, visitor growth may appear not merely desirable but necessary. Yet the people who live in a destination experience tourism as a transformation of housing, streets, work and culture. The question is therefore not whether residents should be consulted, but whether their preferences may legitimately limit an economically valuable industry.

What gives residents a special claim is that they bear the continuous consequences of decisions consumed temporarily by visitors. A traveller may experience congestion for three days; a resident may experience it every morning. A visitor can choose another destination, while a tenant facing displacement cannot relocate without substantial cost. The economic case for growth remains powerful. Tourism can usher in a period of foreign-income growth, support local businesses and provide employment where manufacturing or agriculture has declined. Heritage attractions may require visitor revenue for maintenance. A strict limit can reduce opportunity for young workers and entrepreneurs.

However, aggregate income does not reveal distribution. A hotel owner may gain while renters face higher costs. Tourism leakage may send revenue to outside companies, while municipalities pay for cleaning, transport and policing. Distributional effects determine whether growth improves local welfare.

Housing is the clearest conflict. Short-term rentals and hotel conversion can increase property values but weaken housing affordability. Workers serving tourists may then be unable to live near their jobs. An industry that depends on local labour while removing local housing contains an obvious structural contradiction.

Residents also possess non-market interests. Streets, beaches, religious spaces and festivals carry everyday and cultural meaning. Cultural commodification may transform a tradition into a scheduled performance, while ordinary businesses are crowded out. These losses cannot be measured fully through visitor expenditure. Only when local economic benefits exceed these wider costs can tourism growth claim democratic legitimacy. Determining that balance requires evidence and participation rather than assuming any additional visitor is automatically beneficial.

The right to limit tourism should not mean that current property owners can exclude all change. Residents are not a single group. Hotel workers, tenants, business owners and retired homeowners may have different interests. Some voices are easier to organise and may use heritage language to protect private privilege. Democratic processes must therefore include groups who are often absent from formal meetings: renters, younger residents, seasonal workers and minority communities. A resident consultation process should collect structured evidence rather than reward the people with the most time to attend hearings.

The scale of decision also matters. One neighbourhood may wish to restrict bars or rentals while the wider city benefits from tourism revenue. National governments may promote arrivals while municipalities manage pressure. Authority should sit close enough to the impact, but local decisions must respect broader rights and law.

A legitimate system would grant destinations tools over accommodation, land use, port schedules and public space. Cities could introduce tourism zoning, registration, visitor caps or timed access. These measures should identify a specific harm and include review periods. Were restrictions imposed without measurable objectives, they could become arbitrary barriers benefiting established businesses. A cap that protects incumbent hotels while preventing small local competitors would not represent community welfare.

Visitor fees illustrate the complexity. Charges may reduce demand and provide conservation funding, but they can exclude lower-income travellers. Residents should help decide the amount and use of revenue, while exemptions preserve equitable access. Cruise tourism often strengthens the case for local control. Ships deliver concentrated crowds, emissions and infrastructure demand. Port authorities may earn revenue while historic districts absorb pressure. Municipalities need power to coordinate schedules and ease back on arrivals when capacity is exceeded.

Cultural heritage also justifies limits. Repeated footfall can wear down irreplaceable material, and no future revenue can recreate the original fabric perfectly. A capacity-first precaution is appropriate where damage may be irreversible. Yet tourism can support conservation politically and financially. Sites without visitors may struggle to secure budgets. The objective is not zero access but a model compatible with heritage values. Timed entry, guided routes and seasonal closure can preserve both learning and protection.

Wildlife-based tourism presents the same dilemma. Visitors may finance parks while disturbing wildlife and consuming scarce water. Local and Indigenous communities should possess authority where tourism affects land, culture and livelihoods. Consent cannot be replaced by a distant operator’s claim that visitors create national income. Many destinations have promoted rapid visitor growth for years, yet they have invested too little in housing, transport and conservation capacity. Restriction becomes necessary partly because earlier governance treated marketing as policy.

The stronger approach is adaptive management. Destinations should monitor resident sentiment, site condition, congestion, wages and local ownership. Thresholds can trigger changes to permits, marketing or access before crisis produces hostility. Economic diversification also matters. Communities dependent on tourism may feel unable to refuse growth, even when it damages quality of life. Investment in education, digital services, agriculture or creative industries expands genuine choice. The right to limit tourism is weak if exercising it means immediate economic collapse.

Workers need protection during change. Reducing arrivals can eliminate jobs, so transition funds, training and income support should accompany restrictions. Justice applies both to residents harmed by tourism and employees dependent on it. There is also a responsibility beyond the destination. Travellers and platforms influence pressure. Booking systems can encourage off-season travel, longer stays and less saturated areas. Airlines and cruise companies should provide data and contribute to infrastructure rather than externalising costs.

Had visitor growth been connected earlier to housing and conservation investment, some current restrictions might have been unnecessary. The lesson is that growth should finance capacity before limits are exceeded. Residents should not hold an absolute veto over every visitor or project. Heritage and nature may have national or global significance, and public access matters. Local power must operate within transparent legal principles, including non-discrimination and proportionality. Not only should residents have the right to limit harmful tourism, but they should also have the power to shape the tourism that remains. Participation should determine ownership models, visitor behaviour, revenue use and cultural boundaries.

Tourism policy needs evidence-based policymaking and honest cost-benefit analysis, both directed towards long-term public value. Equitable access to streets and attractions matters to residents, visitors and essential workers serving the destination.

A strong visitor economy invests in human capital. Lifelong learning and transferable skills help seasonal staff progress, while targeted support can make tourism a route towards intergenerational mobility.

Overcrowding and insecure housing can produce chronic stress and harm mental wellbeing. Even residents in secure employment may face structural barriers to housing or basic drinking water when peak demand overwhelms local systems.

Fair regulation recognises individual circumstances and sets an evidence threshold before limiting livelihoods. Hosts and workers need legal safeguards, fewer employment barriers and clear rules that protect public confidence.

Booking platforms should provide algorithmic transparency because rankings create information asymmetry. Strong regulatory oversight and procedural fairness also protect freedom of expression when residents or small firms challenge a decision.

Visitor-data systems should follow data minimisation and a legitimate purpose. Independent oversight can close the accountability gap, while local institutions build up the capacity to enforce licences consistently.

Tourism creates entry-level roles, yet automation should support worker augmentation rather than abrupt job displacement. Businesses receiving public help should provide paid training and share productivity gains with staff.

Heritage management requires funding continuity and scientific independence. Mission-driven research, careful replication studies and open knowledge spillovers can improve conservation without turning one popular intervention into a universal formula.

Crowd management can combine Earth observation and satellite data with ticketing. Climate monitoring, weather forecasting and coordinated disaster response are equally important for exposed coastal and mountain destinations.

Tourism must become part of climate adaptation. Adaptation finance, flood resilience and early-warning systems protect communities, while managed retreat may be necessary where waterfront assets face repeated damage.

Poorly managed visitation can accelerate biodiversity loss and weaken ecosystem services. Protecting soil biodiversity, pursuing nature-positive development and preventing pollinator decline must shape access to sensitive landscapes.

Tourism can support food security through local purchasing, but market concentration often favours large suppliers. Shorter supply chains can reduce food waste and pressure on places already facing water scarcity.

Holiday rentals can deepen housing insecurity, making the land-use trade-off visible. Strong municipal delivery capacity is needed so sustainable urban development can proceed without visitor demand continuing to add to local costs.

A circular economy can reduce tourism's material footprint and reveal hidden economic externalities. Better resource productivity is especially important where seasonal demand widens a regional water-security gap.

International tourism sits within global value-chains and services trade, so trade diversification can reduce dependence on one visitor market. Yet there is no shared trade benefit if residents alone carry the adjustment burden.

Effective tourism governance begins with a coherent tourism strategy. International tourism, domestic tourism and cruise tourism create different patterns of spending, access and pressure, so one numerical target cannot manage them all.

At busy heritage sites, local residents may face crowded streets while new hotel development increases demand. Reliable public transport and protected local services help the same district serve visitors without becoming unusable to its community.

Well-designed visitor centres and small guided tours can explain appropriate behaviour. Wildlife-based tourism requires particular care during peak season, when repeated disturbance can be more damaging than the annual visitor total suggests.

The central destination-policy trade-off concerns income and scarce capacity. Transparent estimates of the visitor-capacity opportunity cost, targeted destination-resilience investment and a visible community tourism dividend should appear in public destination-performance indicators.

Policy should identify resident welfare costs through a visitor-pressure risk appraisal. Destination-governance accountability, a clear short-term-rental regulatory framework and place-based policy support resident-centred growth, credible destination-policy delivery and durable destination competitiveness.

The relevant right is therefore not a right to isolation. It is a right to a liveable place and meaningful control over development. Tourism income is valuable, but income is a means to community wellbeing rather than a reason to override it. When growth makes a destination less habitable, residents are justified in saying that enough visitors are enough.

Exam-length model

6. Realistic IELTS essay · approximately 300 words

Question: Some people believe popular tourist destinations should limit visitor numbers, while others argue that restrictions would harm local economies. Discuss both views and give your opinion.
Model answer · 320 words

Popular destinations increasingly use booking systems, fees and visitor limits to manage congestion. Supporters believe these measures protect residents and heritage, while critics fear lost employment and income. In my view, targeted limits are justified where tourism exceeds local capacity, but they should form part of a broader destination strategy.

The economic argument against restrictions is strong. Tourism supports hotels, restaurants, transport and cultural institutions. Visitor spending may sustain areas with few alternative industries. Sudden caps can reduce tourism jobs and damage small businesses that invested in expected demand. What local economies need is predictable management rather than abrupt closure. However, unlimited growth creates costs. Crowds wear down heritage, overwhelm transport and reduce resident access to public space. Tourist accommodation may crowd out long-term housing. Only when visitor numbers remain within carrying capacity can tourism produce durable benefits.

Limits should therefore be specific. Fragile sites can require visitors to book ahead or use timed entry, while ports coordinate cruise schedules. Cities can regulate short-term rentals and introduce seasonal pricing. These measures are better than a single citywide ceiling because pressure varies across time and place.

Revenue must support those affected. Visitor fees should fund conservation, services and worker transition. Destinations should also encourage longer stays and off-season travel, allowing spending to spread more widely. Many places have promoted arrivals aggressively, yet infrastructure and housing investment have remained insufficient. Economic diversification also matters because destinations dependent on one season or attraction have little freedom to limit growth. Support for local food, creative industries and remote services can protect income when visitor numbers are reduced. Residents should participate in setting limits and reviewing results. Had local capacity expanded alongside tourism, severe restrictions might not now be required.

In conclusion, visitor limits can harm businesses if imposed carelessly, but unmanaged overtourism damages the economic and cultural assets on which tourism depends. Evidence-based caps, transparent revenue and diversification can protect both destinations and livelihoods.

Why the exam-length essay is strong

Direct position

The introduction accepts tourism income while making resident wellbeing a condition of legitimate growth.

Causal explanation

The essay connects visitor concentration to housing, crowding, heritage wear and local business value.

Developed contrast

Economic opportunity is weighed against visible pressure on residents, services and fragile sites.

Policy mechanism

Timed entry, visitor levies, rental rules and seasonal dispersal turn the position into a workable programme.

Recycled language

Earlier collocations return as part of the reasoning rather than as decoration.

Controlled complexity

Advanced grammar remains clear enough for realistic exam conditions.

7. Advanced grammar transformations

1. If cities had limited rentals earlier, fewer residents would have been displaced. (Past-perfect conditional)

Had cities limited rentals earlier, fewer residents would have been displaced.

2. Visitor limits work only when they are enforced consistently. (Negative inversion)

Only when visitor limits are enforced consistently do they work.

3. Resident wellbeing matters most in destination policy. (Cleft sentence)

What matters most in destination policy is resident wellbeing.

4. Tourism should create income and preserve heritage. (Balanced recommendation)

Tourism should both create income and preserve heritage.

5. The site was designed for worship, but it became a crowded attraction. (Participle clause)

Designed for worship, the site became a crowded attraction.

6. Although visitor fees are useful, they cannot solve housing pressure. (Fronted concession)

Useful though visitor fees may be, they cannot solve housing pressure.

7. Tourism supports businesses and finances conservation. (Not only...but also)

Not only does tourism support businesses, but it also finances conservation.

8. Cities have promoted tourism, but infrastructure has remained weak. (Present-perfect contrast)

Cities have promoted tourism, yet infrastructure has remained weak.

9. The authority introduced booking after the site had suffered damage. (Past perfect)

The authority introduced booking after the site had suffered damage.

10. The tourism agency lacks reliable data, so policy remains reactive. (Nominalisation)

The tourism agency’s lack of reliable data keeps policy reactive.

11. If visitors stayed longer, local spending might rise. (Conditional inversion)

Local spending might rise were visitors to stay longer.

12. Residents opposed the project because consultation was absent. (Cleft cause)

It was the absence of consultation that caused residents to oppose the project.

13. Destinations should manage crowds, protect housing and support workers. (Controlled parallelism)

Destinations should manage crowds, protect housing and support workers.

14. The city introduced the tourist tax gradually, so businesses could adapt. (Participle clause)

Introducing the tourist tax gradually, the city gave businesses time to adapt.

15. Managers changed the route after conservation staff reported damage. (Emphatic do)

Managers did change the route after conservation staff reported damage.

16. No factor matters more than protecting irreplaceable heritage. (Negative inversion)

Rarely does any factor matter more than protecting irreplaceable heritage.

17. If local businesses captured more value, communities would benefit more. (Conditional inversion)

Communities would benefit more were local businesses to capture more value.

18. Tourism should be profitable, inclusive and sustainable. (Controlled parallelism)

Tourism should support profitability, widen inclusion and preserve sustainability.

8. Native Academic Toolbox

1. Upgrade: “There are too many tourists.” using overtourism.

The destination is experiencing overtourism because visitor pressure exceeds local capacity.

2. Upgrade: “Tourists arrive at the same time.” using peak-season demand.

Peak-season demand concentrates pressure within a narrow period.

3. Upgrade: “The city wants visitors in other areas.” using spatial dispersal.

Spatial dispersal can redirect visitors towards less congested districts.

4. Upgrade: “Residents are angry about tourism.” using resident sentiment.

Declining resident sentiment indicates that tourism’s social costs are increasing.

5. Upgrade: “Tourist apartments reduce normal housing.” using local displacement.

Tourist accommodation contributes to local displacement and housing pressure.

6. Upgrade: “Tourism money leaves the area.” using tourism leakage.

Tourism leakage reduces the share of visitor spending retained locally.

7. Upgrade: “The monument is being damaged.” using heritage fabric.

Heavy footfall is damaging the monument’s heritage fabric.

8. Upgrade: “The site needs fewer visitors.” using visitor caps.

Visitor caps may be necessary where conservation thresholds are exceeded.

9. Upgrade: “The city charges tourists.” using tourist taxes.

Tourist taxes can finance services and conservation if revenue is transparent.

10. Upgrade: “Tourism needs better management.” using destination stewardship.

Destination stewardship integrates resident welfare, heritage and visitor quality.

11. Upgrade: “Tourism is only busy in summer.” using seasonal employment.

Seasonal employment creates income insecurity and recruitment problems.

12. Upgrade: “The destination relies too much on visitors.” using tourism dependence.

Tourism dependence increases vulnerability to external shocks.

13. Upgrade: “Local people should decide.” using community consent.

Community consent should guide tourism development and cultural use.

14. Upgrade: “Visitors should stay longer.” using local value-capture.

Longer stays may improve local value-capture through wider spending.

15. Upgrade: “Tourism and housing policy should match.” using policy coherence.

Policy coherence is essential across tourism, housing and transport.

9. IELTS Speaking

Part 1 · 15 questions

PART 1 · 1

Do you enjoy visiting famous places?

Suggested phrasal verbs
book aheadspace out
Model answer · 82 words

I enjoy famous places when they still feel like real environments rather than queues organised around photographs. I am willing to book ahead for a fragile site if the system is simple and fair. I also try to space out my visit by arriving early or travelling outside peak periods. The experience is usually better when I can understand the history instead of concentrating on how quickly the crowd is moving. Advance information about expected crowd levels would help visitors choose responsibly.

PART 1 · 2

Would you visit a destination in the off-season?

Suggested phrasal verbs
mount upstay over
Model answer · 85 words

I would travel in the off-season because prices, transport and public spaces are often calmer. Bookings can mount up more evenly by offering cultural events beyond summer. I would also be more likely to stay over rather than rush through a crowded day trip. The drawback is uncertain weather and reduced opening hours, so businesses need accurate information rather than advertising an off-season experience that barely exists. Local transport should also operate outside peak season if dispersal is a serious policy rather than a slogan.

PART 1 · 3

Have tourists changed your city?

Suggested phrasal verbs
crowd outusher in
Model answer · 82 words

Tourists have supported restaurants, museums and transport, and they can usher in a period of higher local income that would not exist otherwise. However, visitor-oriented businesses can crowd out ordinary shops in central areas. The impact depends on scale and neighbourhood. A few successful cafés improve a district, while streets containing only luggage storage, souvenir shops and temporary apartments become less useful to residents. Resident surveys can reveal changes that visitor-spending statistics completely miss. Housing data should be included alongside visitor statistics.

PART 1 · 4

Would you pay a tourist tax?

Suggested phrasal verbs
roll in graduallygive back
Model answer · 85 words

I would pay a modest tourist tax if the money was clearly given back to the place through cleaning, transport or conservation. Authorities should roll charges in gradually and transparently and publish results. I would object if the fee simply disappeared into a general budget while residents still faced overcrowding. Visitors use public infrastructure, so a contribution is reasonable, but the amount should remain proportionate and easy to understand. Digital payment should not be the only option for older visitors or people without international cards.

PART 1 · 5

Do you prefer guided tours or exploring alone?

Suggested phrasal verbs
diversify intomove away
Model answer · 80 words

I like exploring alone, although a knowledgeable guide can reveal details I would miss. Good tours help visitors diversify into experiences beyond one famous monument and move away from superficial facts. Large groups can block streets and reduce the atmosphere, so smaller tours and audio guides may work better in fragile centres. The best format depends on whether the purpose is orientation, detailed history or simple entertainment. Guides should be trained and paid fairly because interpretation is skilled cultural work.

PART 1 · 6

Would you use a timed-entry system?

Suggested phrasal verbs
book aheadcap at
Model answer · 79 words

I would use timed entry when a site cannot safely manage unlimited crowds. Visitors may need to book ahead, while managers cap visits at a level compatible with safety and conservation. The system should reserve some tickets for people without digital access or long-term travel plans. A perfectly efficient app is not equitable if it turns public heritage into something available only to fast, well-informed users. Cancellation and refund rules should remain clear when weather or site conditions change.

PART 1 · 7

Do cruise ships benefit port cities?

Suggested phrasal verbs
usher inease back on
Model answer · 83 words

Cruise ships usher in large numbers of visitors and support transport, guiding and retail. However, arrivals are concentrated, and many passengers spend only a few hours ashore. Cities may need to ease back on ship numbers or coordinate schedules when streets and ports are overloaded. The economic benefit should be measured against pollution, infrastructure and the fact that cruise companies retain much of the visitor’s spending. Port fees can support cleaner infrastructure, but they should not be presented as a complete environmental solution.

PART 1 · 8

Would you avoid an overcrowded destination?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move awaydiversify into
Model answer · 84 words

I would avoid a place during its busiest period because overcrowding reduces both comfort and respect for residents. I might move away from the obvious route and diversify my trip into visits to nearby towns with similar culture. This should not become a hunt for the next undiscovered place to overwhelm. Tourism boards need to prepare secondary destinations with transport, information and community support before sending large numbers there. Smaller places should retain the right to limit promotion when infrastructure is already under strain.

PART 1 · 9

Are visitor fees fair?

Suggested phrasal verbs
usher ingive back
Model answer · 81 words

Visitor fees are fair when they usher in new funding for services that tourists use and give back to conservation or local communities. They become unfair when prices exclude lower-income visitors from publicly significant heritage. Discounts, free days and resident access can balance these goals. Authorities should also show whether the fee reduces crowding or merely charges people for experiencing the same congestion. Fee income should be independently audited so conservation claims can be checked.

Clear concessions can keep access fair.

PART 1 · 10

Do holiday rentals affect neighbourhoods?

Suggested phrasal verbs
crowd outcrack down
Model answer · 83 words

Large numbers of holiday rentals can crowd out long-term residents and change building management. Occasional home sharing is different from professional investors operating many units. Cities should crack down on illegal commercial activity while protecting genuine residents who rent a room sometimes. Platform data and registration are necessary because rules cannot work when authorities do not know which properties are repeatedly used for tourism. Enforcement should protect tenants without encouraging hidden or discriminatory rental practices.

Stable registration also helps neighbours report repeated problems.

PART 1 · 11

Would you stay longer in one destination?

Suggested phrasal verbs
stay overspace out
Model answer · 82 words

I prefer staying longer because it reduces travel stress and allows spending to space out across different businesses and neighbourhoods. When visitors stay over, they may use restaurants, cultural events and local transport rather than taking one photograph and leaving. Longer stays can still create pressure, but they usually support deeper understanding and higher local spending than a sequence of extremely short visits. Longer trips may also reduce the environmental cost per day when transport is the largest part of the footprint.

PART 1 · 12

Should tourists follow local dress rules?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move awaygive back
Model answer · 83 words

Tourists should respect dress and behaviour rules in religious or culturally sensitive places. Travel should move away from the idea that payment grants unlimited personal freedom. Respect is one small way visitors give back by reducing conflict and treating a community as more than scenery. Rules should be explained clearly, because visitors may not understand local expectations without guidance. Visitors should follow photography restrictions as well as clothing rules. Respect also includes noise, queues and ordinary residential space.

Clear signs prevent avoidable misunderstandings.

PART 1 · 13

Do social media influence where you travel?

Suggested phrasal verbs
mount upcrowd out
Model answer · 83 words

Intense interest in one viewpoint, street or beach can mount up through social media within weeks. This may crowd out ordinary use and create dangerous behaviour as people copy the same image. I use online recommendations, but I try to compare them with practical information and local sources. A destination is usually more interesting than the single location selected by an algorithm. Platforms could reduce pressure by promoting different times instead of rewarding the most crowded image.

I also avoid copying risky behaviour.

PART 1 · 14

Would you visit a heritage site under restoration?

Suggested phrasal verbs
wear downusher in
Model answer · 82 words

I would visit if access was safe and did not interfere with conservation work. Tourism can wear down historic surfaces, but controlled visits may usher in funding and help people understand restoration. Sites should explain what is being repaired and why, rather than hiding all work behind decorative screens. Seeing conservation can make heritage feel like an active responsibility instead of a finished product. Restoration schedules should prioritise the site rather than visitor convenience.

That transparency makes temporary disruption easier to accept.

PART 1 · 15

Should local residents enter attractions for free?

Suggested phrasal verbs
give backroll in gradually
Model answer · 82 words

Free or reduced resident access can give back something to communities that live with tourism pressure. Authorities could roll local passes in gradually for museums and heritage sites, especially where public money supports them. Residents should not feel that their own cultural spaces have become products designed entirely for outsiders. The policy still needs fair verification and funding, but the principle is reasonable. Schools and community groups could receive priority access during quieter periods.

This can rebuild a sense of local ownership.

Part 3 · 15 questions

PART 3 · 1

What causes overtourism?

Suggested phrasal verbs
mount upcrowd out
Model answer · 119 words

Overtourism develops when visitor numbers mount up faster than housing, transport and governance can adapt. Cheap flights, cruise schedules, social media and concentrated marketing direct people toward the same places and times. Tourist accommodation may crowd out residents, while local services shift toward visitors. The problem is not simply the number of tourists; it is the mismatch between flows and carrying capacity. Better data, land-use rules and regional coordination are needed because isolated restrictions merely move pressure between streets or neighbouring towns. Destination marketing should be reduced before emergency restrictions become the only remaining tool. National governments should also give municipalities legal tools over accommodation, transport and promotion, since local authorities often carry the pressure without controlling its causes.

PART 3 · 2

Should cities limit tourist numbers?

Suggested phrasal verbs
cap atroll in gradually
Model answer · 124 words

Cities should use limits where specific sites or districts face clear physical, environmental or social harm. Managers can cap visits at a safe level and roll booking or permit systems in gradually. However, a citywide numerical ceiling may be crude because pressure varies by time and location. Limits should be linked to evidence on congestion, housing and heritage condition. Authorities must also protect access for residents, workers and lower-income visitors. Restrictions are credible when they solve a defined problem rather than merely create an exclusive destination. Capacity should include resident tolerance and service quality, not merely how many bodies fit inside a space. Review periods matter because visitor patterns, infrastructure and climate conditions change, making one permanent cap an unreliable substitute for continuous management.

PART 3 · 3

Are tourist taxes effective?

Suggested phrasal verbs
usher ingive back
Model answer · 123 words

Tourist taxes can usher in new funding for cleaning, public transport and conservation funding. They can also ensure visitors give back to places whose infrastructure they use. Yet a small tax rarely changes travel demand, and revenue may disappear into general budgets. Effectiveness depends on transparency, rate design and local authority. Higher charges during peak periods may encourage seasonal dispersal, while exemptions can protect necessary travel. Residents should participate in deciding how revenue is spent, especially when tourism affects housing and public space. Public dashboards can show whether revenue reaches neighbourhoods carrying the greatest pressure. Charges can be differentiated by season, vehicle type or accommodation class, provided the system remains simple enough to administer and explain.

The rate should also be reviewed regularly.

PART 3 · 4

How can heritage sites protect themselves from crowds?

Suggested phrasal verbs
book aheadwear down
Model answer · 124 words

Fragile sites can require visitors to book ahead, use timed entry and follow defined routes. These measures prevent simultaneous peaks and reduce the repeated contact that can wear down historic surfaces. Guides, barriers and interpretation should direct behaviour without turning the site into an airport queue. Monitoring must track physical condition, not only ticket numbers. Some areas may need seasonal closure or complete exclusion. Digital access and replicas can widen learning, but they should complement rather than replace meaningful public access. Conservation staff need authority to close areas when monitoring shows deterioration. Staff numbers and maintenance budgets must rise with visitor demand; technology cannot protect heritage when conservation teams remain chronically underfunded. Visitor routes should also protect emergency access and ordinary movement for residents.

PART 3 · 5

Does tourism preserve or commercialise culture?

Suggested phrasal verbs
usher inmove away
Model answer · 128 words

Tourism can usher in a period of renewed funding for crafts, festivals and heritage conservation, giving traditions economic support. It can also simplify culture into performances designed for visitor expectations. Destinations should move away from judging success only through sales and attendance. Community control matters because residents should decide which practices may be shared and how. Cultural change is normal, so authenticity should not mean freezing people in the past. The problem is unequal power when commercial operators define culture while local participants receive little income or authority. Contracts with commercial performers should protect ownership, attribution and fair payment. Schools and local cultural organisations should retain access to spaces and traditions that tourism businesses increasingly monetise. Local museums can present changing traditions without reducing them to fixed performances.

PART 3 · 6

Should cruise tourism be restricted?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ease back onspace out
Model answer · 125 words

Ports should ease back on cruise arrivals when simultaneous ships overwhelm streets, transport or air quality. Scheduling can space out passengers across days, while port fees finance infrastructure. Cruise tourism creates jobs, but much spending remains on board and visits are extremely concentrated. Restrictions should reflect local evidence rather than hostility toward one type of traveller. Cleaner ships and shore power reduce some environmental effects, yet they do not solve crowding or housing pressure. Cities need authority to coordinate port capacity with destination management. Cruise companies should share passenger-flow data early enough for cities to plan. Shore power, cleaner fuels and smaller vessels can reduce some environmental harm, but destination capacity must still determine arrival schedules.

Resident monitoring can reveal pressures missed by port data.

PART 3 · 7

Can visitor dispersal solve overtourism?

Suggested phrasal verbs
space outdiversify into
Model answer · 121 words

Visitor dispersal can space out demand and help travellers diversify into activities beyond famous landmarks. It may support rural or secondary destinations and reduce pressure at one site. However, promotion can export overcrowding to communities that lack infrastructure or consent. Dispersal works only when transport, waste services, heritage protection and local businesses are prepared. Some popular places also need absolute reductions rather than redistribution. Marketing agencies should share data and allow destinations to decline promotion when growth conflicts with resident welfare. Secondary destinations also need the right to control branding and visitor growth. Cooperation between neighbouring destinations can prevent competitive promotion from recreating the same problem across an entire region. Data should track displacement and infrastructure pressure in the promoted destinations.

PART 3 · 8

How do short-term rentals affect tourism cities?

Suggested phrasal verbs
crowd outcrack down
Model answer · 128 words

Commercial short-term rentals can crowd out long-term housing and encourage landlords to prioritise visitor revenue. Buildings lose stable residents, while local shops and schools may decline. Cities should crack down on unlicensed portfolios through registration, platform data and meaningful penalties. Nevertheless, home sharing can provide household income and flexible accommodation. Regulation should distinguish a primary residence rented occasionally from investment properties operating as hotels. Tourism policy and housing policy must be integrated because each agency otherwise treats the same apartment differently. Housing enforcement should coordinate with tax, safety and tenant-protection agencies. Rental regulation should also monitor hotel conversion and serviced apartments, since pressure does not originate from one platform or accommodation type. Fines must be large enough that illegal operation is not simply treated as a business expense.

PART 3 · 9

Should famous natural sites charge entry fees?

Suggested phrasal verbs
usher incap at
Model answer · 125 words

Entry fees can usher in a new funding stream for paths, toilets, safety and ecosystem protection. Booking can also cap visits at levels that reduce erosion and congestion. The difficulty is equitable access to landscapes often regarded as public heritage. Resident discounts, free walking access or low-income concessions can preserve fairness. Fees should reflect actual management needs and remain transparent. Charging every visitor without improving infrastructure merely monetises overcrowding. Authorities should also consider whether vehicle or parking fees target pressure more accurately than individual entry. Indigenous and local custodians should help design fees where cultural or natural rights are involved. Fees should not replace ordinary public funding for protected areas, because conservation obligations continue even when visitor demand temporarily falls.

Booking rules should remain simple.

PART 3 · 10

How can tourism benefit local communities?

Suggested phrasal verbs
give backstay over
Model answer · 126 words

Tourism benefits communities when visitors stay over, buy from locally owned firms and support stable employment. Revenue should be given back through public services, heritage funds and community projects. Procurement can connect hotels with local food and crafts, while training helps residents enter management rather than only seasonal service jobs. Land ownership matters because external investors may capture most value. Community consent and access to decision-making are therefore as important as total spending. Benefits should be measured across income groups, not inferred from destination growth. Local-value capture should include ownership, wages and supply contracts rather than souvenir sales alone. Tourism boards can publish local ownership and wage indicators, making economic benefit more visible than headline visitor expenditure.

Cooperatives can help residents retain ownership and bargaining power.

PART 3 · 11

Is off-season tourism always beneficial?

Suggested phrasal verbs
mount upspace out
Model answer · 118 words

Off-season travel can prevent seasonal income gaps from mounting up and space out pressure across the year. It may use hotels and transport that would otherwise remain idle. However, ecosystems and communities also need recovery periods, and some businesses prefer closure for maintenance or family life. Climate conditions may make promotion unsafe or unrealistic. Policy should identify what capacity genuinely exists outside peak season instead of turning every month into a commercial opportunity. Stable tourism is not the same as continuous maximum occupancy. Employment programmes should reduce seasonal insecurity rather than simply extending low-paid work across more months. Destinations should protect worker rest, housing and training so a longer season improves employment quality rather than only business revenue.

PART 3 · 12

Can technology manage tourism better?

Suggested phrasal verbs
book aheadspace out
Model answer · 123 words

Digital tools allow visitors to book ahead, receive crowd information and space out across routes or time slots. Mobile data can reveal pressure patterns, while smart tickets manage access. Yet technology creates privacy, accessibility and platform-dependence concerns. Data should be governed publicly, and offline options must remain available. An app cannot solve a housing shortage or weak enforcement. Technology is most useful when it supports a clear destination strategy rather than substituting for political decisions about acceptable growth. Open algorithms and independent audits can reduce manipulation by dominant booking platforms. Public agencies should retain ownership of essential visitor-flow data rather than becoming dependent on commercial platforms for basic planning. Digital systems should be tested with older users and international visitors before mandatory use.

PART 3 · 13

How does tourism affect climate and nature?

Suggested phrasal verbs
ease back onwear down
Model answer · 118 words

Tourism produces transport emissions, water demand, waste and habitat disturbance. Destinations may need to ease back on activities that damage sensitive ecosystems, while heavy use can wear down paths, beaches and historic structures. The sector also finances parks and creates incentives to protect scenery. Climate change threatens the same assets through heat, fire, erosion and sea-level rise. Tourism planning should therefore combine emission reduction, climate adaptation and visitor management. Environmental costs must be measured across the entire journey, not only at the hotel. Visitor education should explain restrictions so conservation is understood rather than treated as inconvenience. Cruise, aviation and accommodation policies should be assessed together because one efficient hotel does not cancel the emissions of the journey.

PART 3 · 14

Should governments promote lesser-known destinations?

Suggested phrasal verbs
diversify intomove away
Model answer · 124 words

Governments can help visitors diversify into new regional experiences and support economic diversification in rural or secondary regions. They should move away from promoting famous places that are already saturated. However, new destinations need local consent, transport, waste systems and protection before major campaigns begin. Marketing can create demand much faster than governance creates capacity. Success should be defined through local income, stable jobs and place quality, not the number of social-media impressions or coaches arriving during one season. Funding should continue after promotional grants end, since infrastructure and conservation require maintenance. Smaller destinations should be supported in developing products gradually, with clear thresholds for pausing growth when resident or environmental indicators worsen. Regional branding should avoid promising tranquillity while actively engineering rapid growth.

PART 3 · 15

What would sustainable tourism look like?

Suggested phrasal verbs
give backmove away
Model answer · 128 words

Sustainable tourism would move away from arrival numbers as the main measure of success. Visitors would stay longer, use lower-impact transport and give back through local spending and conservation contributions. Residents would influence development, retain access to housing and public spaces, and receive stable work. Heritage and ecosystems would set limits on growth. Tourism could remain economically important, but destinations would diversify so one shock did not collapse the local economy. The goal is a good place to live that remains worth visiting. Success also requires reliable data on resident sentiment, biodiversity and cultural continuity. A resilient destination would also preserve everyday services, schools and housing so tourism remains part of local life rather than replacing it.

Success should be judged through resident wellbeing as well as revenue.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

Cities should ban new short-term tourist rentals in neighbourhoods with severe housing shortages. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
holiday rentalscrowd outcrack downovertourismtourism intensityvisitor pressurecarrying capacityvisitor managementdestination management
Model essay · 323 words

Short-term rentals provide flexible accommodation and household income, but they can remove homes from already constrained markets. I agree that cities should ban new commercial tourist rentals in neighbourhoods with severe shortages, although genuine home sharing should remain possible.

The main justification is housing. Investors may convert apartments into holiday rentals because nightly rates exceed long-term rent. This can crowd out residents and weaken housing affordability. What the market rewards in a tourist district is temporary occupancy rather than community stability.

A geographically targeted ban is preferable to a citywide rule. Pressure differs among neighbourhoods, and some areas have sufficient capacity. Authorities should use rental, vacancy and resident data to define saturated zones. Existing operators should be registered and safety standards enforced.

The policy should distinguish a primary residence rented occasionally from businesses controlling multiple properties. Cities can crack down on unlicensed portfolios through platform data and substantial fines. Only when enforcement is credible will compliant operators avoid being undercut by illegal supply. Critics argue that bans reduce tourism income and property rights. These concerns are legitimate, so existing legal businesses may need transition periods. Hotels and purpose-built accommodation can serve visitors without removing the same quantity of housing. Rental restrictions must accompany new construction, tenant protection and social housing. A ban cannot solve every shortage, but it can stop tourism demand from worsening a particularly fragile market. The city should review the boundary regularly, since housing conditions and tourism demand change. Public data can prevent a temporary emergency measure from becoming an unquestioned permanent restriction. Enforcement should coordinate housing, tax and safety agencies so illegal operators cannot exploit gaps between departments. Tenant organisations should also be able to report suspected commercial conversion without beginning an expensive legal case.

In conclusion: banning new commercial short-term rentals in severely pressured neighbourhoods is justified. The rule should remain targeted, evidence-based and compatible with occasional home sharing rather than treating every resident as a hotel company.

TASK 2 · 2

Some people believe tourist taxes are an effective way to manage overtourism, while others think they merely make travel more expensive. Discuss both views and give your opinion.

Optional collocation bank
usher ingive backvisitor-behaviour nudgesoff-season travelequitable accessday visitorsovertourismtourism intensityvisitor pressure
Model essay · 314 words

Tourist taxes are widely used to make visitors contribute to local infrastructure. Supporters also hope that charges will reduce excessive demand, while critics view them as an additional cost with little effect. In my view, taxes are useful for funding management but rarely solve overtourism alone.

The strongest benefit is revenue. Visitors use transport, cleaning, public space and heritage sites, so a tax can usher in a funding stream that is given back to the destination. Revenue may support conservation, resident services or visitor information. What a tourist tax can correct is the gap between private tourism income and public management costs.

Taxes can also vary by season or accommodation type, creating visitor-behaviour nudges. Higher peak charges may encourage off-season travel. However, small fees are unlikely to change demand for famous destinations, while high fees can reduce equitable access. Effectiveness depends on transparency. Authorities should publish collection and spending data. Not only must visitors pay the charge, but residents must also see a measurable improvement. Without this link, the tax becomes ordinary revenue accompanied by sustainable-tourism language.

Taxes should be combined with accommodation rules, booking systems and transport planning. A city cannot tax unlimited arrivals and then claim the pressure has been managed. Lower-income travellers and necessary visitors may need exemptions. Digital collection should also avoid excessive administrative costs for small accommodation providers. Accommodation platforms can collect the tax automatically, but municipalities should retain access to transaction data for auditing and planning. Privacy safeguards remain necessary. The tax design should consider day visitors and cruise passengers, who may use infrastructure without paying an accommodation levy. Port charges or attraction fees can distribute responsibility more fairly.

In conclusion: tourist taxes are effective as a financing instrument and may modestly influence timing. They are not a substitute for visitor limits, housing policy or destination management, but they can support those measures when revenue use is transparent.

TASK 2 · 3

Historic sites are increasingly introducing timed-entry tickets and compulsory advance booking. Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
cap atsite conservationbook aheadwear downovertourismtourism intensityvisitor pressurecarrying capacityvisitor management
Model essay · 314 words

Timed entry and advance booking are becoming common at crowded historic sites. Although these systems reduce spontaneous access, their advantages generally outweigh the disadvantages where heritage is fragile.

The main benefit is controlled flow. Managers can cap visits at a level compatible with safety and site conservation. Visitors who book ahead arrive across defined periods, reducing queues and intense peaks. What timed entry protects is both the physical site and the quality of interpretation.

Advance data also helps managers schedule guides, security and maintenance. Emergency access becomes easier, and visitors spend less time waiting. Heavy footfall can wear down floors, paths and archaeological material, so prevention is cheaper than repair. The disadvantages concern flexibility and fairness. Travellers may not know their exact schedule, while tickets can sell out through resellers or automated systems. People without digital access may be excluded. Were every ticket released online months in advance, public heritage could become accessible mainly to organised and technologically confident visitors.

Sites should reserve same-day tickets, offer telephone or physical booking and provide fair cancellation rules. School groups, residents and low-income visitors may need protected access. Systems should also prevent speculative resale. Timed entry is unnecessary where capacity is ample, so restrictions should follow monitoring rather than fashion. Visitor centres should explain why limits exist. People are more likely to respect restrictions when conservation evidence is visible rather than hidden behind a generic capacity message. Managers should publish attendance, deterioration and visitor-satisfaction data. This allows the public to see whether restrictions protect the site or merely create scarcity that supports higher ticket prices. Ticket design should avoid forcing every visitor through commercial tour packages. Independent travellers should retain access under the same conservation rules.

In conclusion: compulsory booking is justified at fragile or severely crowded sites. Its conservation and safety benefits outweigh reduced spontaneity when access channels remain inclusive and the capacity limit is evidence-based.

TASK 2 · 4

Many tourism jobs are seasonal, insecure and poorly paid. What problems does this create, and how can governments and businesses improve employment quality?

Optional collocation bank
mount upovertourismtourism intensityvisitor pressurecarrying capacityvisitor managementdestination managementtourism governancetourism strategy
Model essay · 311 words

Tourism provides millions of jobs, but employment is often seasonal, insecure and low paid. This creates worker poverty, staff shortages and weak service quality.

Seasonal workers may face months without income, limited training and unstable housing. Employers repeatedly recruit new staff instead of developing experienced teams. What appears as labour flexibility for businesses becomes financial uncertainty for workers. Destinations may also struggle to retain teachers, healthcare staff and other residents when tourism wages cannot meet housing costs.

Governments can improve standards through contracts, minimum wages, predictable schedules and inspection. Benefits and unemployment insurance should recognise seasonal work patterns. Businesses should provide training that helps staff move into supervision, digital marketing or heritage interpretation. Coordination costs can mount up as destinations add off-season events, but extending the season should not simply extend insecure work. Not only should employment last longer, but job quality should also improve. Collective bargaining and worker representation can identify realistic schedules and safety needs.

Housing is part of employment policy. Large employers may support accommodation, while municipalities protect long-term rental supply. However, workers should not become dependent on an employer for both income and insecure housing. Economic diversification creates alternatives and strengthens bargaining power. Training should be portable across hospitality, retail and other services. Public procurement can favour employers offering year-round contracts and accredited training. Tourism boards should report job quality, not only the number of positions supported. Employers also need predictable immigration and seasonal-worker rules where local labour is insufficient. Recruitment should not depend on workers accepting unsafe housing or deductions they cannot challenge. Resident surveys and worker-retention data can show whether employment reform improves the destination rather than merely filling vacancies for another season.

In conclusion: poor tourism employment creates instability for workers and destinations. Stronger labour standards, career development, housing policy and a more balanced annual economy can improve both job quality and visitor service.

TASK 2 · 5

Why do some destinations become dependent on tourism? How can they diversify their economies without destroying the tourism sector?

Optional collocation bank
usher intourism dependencetourism leakagediversify intoovertourismtourism intensityvisitor pressurecarrying capacityvisitor management
Model essay · 306 words

Destinations become dependent on tourism when natural or cultural attractions generate faster returns than alternative industries. Governments and investors then concentrate infrastructure, skills and marketing around visitors.

Tourism may replace declining agriculture or manufacturing and usher in a period of foreign-income growth. Over time, land, labour and credit shift toward hotels and services. Tourism dependence deepens when local tax revenue also relies on arrivals. What begins as diversification can eventually become a new form of concentration. Dependence is risky because pandemics, conflict, climate events or changing consumer tastes can reduce demand suddenly. Seasonal work and tourism leakage may also limit broad prosperity.

Diversification should build on existing capabilities rather than attack tourism. Regions can diversify into food production, creative industries, education, remote services or conservation. Local procurement connects hotels with agriculture and crafts, allowing visitor demand to support other sectors. Education and digital infrastructure are essential. Many destinations have invested heavily in promotion, yet they have underinvested in skills usable outside tourism. Development finance can support small firms whose customers include both residents and visitors.

Tourism itself should become more locally embedded through ownership, stable jobs and longer stays. This reduces leakage while other sectors grow. Diversification takes time, so governments need multi-year plans and social protection. Abruptly restricting tourism before alternatives exist would harm workers. Universities and vocational institutions can connect tourism skills with business management, languages, conservation and digital services. This gives workers options without treating their existing experience as worthless. Land-use policy should protect space for residents and non-tourism businesses. Otherwise, even successful diversification programmes struggle because commercial property and housing have already been reorganised around visitors.

In conclusion: destinations become dependent when visitor activity dominates investment and employment. They can diversify through skills, local supply chains and new industries while maintaining a better-managed tourism sector as one important part of the economy.

Course index · Module 1 · Chapter 16

Academic English Studio · Topic 16 · Tourism, Overtourism and Host Communities
